next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 10

The milk was going to be a problem.

Yor held the carton at an angle under the kitchen light and read the date stamp. Tomorrow. Back into the refrigerator it went, behind the leftover stew, where Loid was less likely to discover it. He had opinions about expiration dates that she considered disproportionate to the actual science of dairy spoilage, but she'd never found a way to say that without sounding like she was defending spoiled milk, which she wasn't. What she was defending was the principle that milk didn't become poison at midnight on the printed date. She was fairly sure about this.

Bond was on the living room rug, occupying approximately one-third of the available floor space with the unselfconscious entitlement of a very large, very old dog who had decided that this particular section of rug was his and always had been. His breathing was slow and even, and his legs twitched with whatever passed through a precognitive dog's sleeping mind. Yor sometimes caught Anya watching Bond with an expression that suggested she was listening to something Yor couldn't hear, but the habit was so old and so gentle that Yor had filed it years ago under the broad category of things about her daughter that she loved without understanding.

A bento box sat open on the counter. This was not strictly necessary. Loid was at the university for a consulting session, one of the occasional appointments that maintained a cover identity that had long since stopped feeling like one, and he wouldn't be back until evening. But Yor liked making them. The routine was grounding. Rice...
open_hooks_to_carry: Assassination attempt: two military-trained assailants, real violence, real injuries
Anya's combat training visible — she is her parents' daughter
The attack may connect to the mole — intelligence chains that touch Anya's false report
Safe house aftermath: wound care, adrenaline drop, proximity
Childhood memories and genuine laughter leading to a kiss
Anya's inability to stop reading Damian's thoughts during intimacy — the moral weight of unearned access
Post-kiss: Damian's thoughts about Anya Forger specifically nearly surface at full resolution — the name is right there

payoffs_to_land.description: The guilt of reading Damian's unguarded feelings during an intimate moment crystallizes the moral asymmetry seeded in Ch 3
The recognition is now imminent — Damian is thinking of Anya Forger by name, though he hasn't committed to the identification yet

prev_tail_excerpt:
...denly needed odd hours, who went too quiet at the wrong moment. Quiet work. Ugly work. Work she could do because Loid had taught her how to build a pattern and because telepathy, filthy unfair telepathy, had dropped the first thread in her lap.

Across the room, Damian found her. Concern first. Then restraint.

*She's off. What happened. Later. Not in here.*

Anya gave him the smallest nod she could manage.

Then she scanned the ballroom again, and the room did not feel warm anymore. It felt searchable.

The general at the buffet was still thinking about shrimp. Tail, sauce, lemon, bite. Not him.

The young diplomat by the window had deleted the breakup text and was now drafting tomorrow's apology. Not her.

Yuri checked the cracked watch Yor had given him years ago and thought, with deep private irritation, that he still couldn't remember whether he'd promised Thursday or Friday dinner. Not him.

A server worried about the tram schedule. A trade official worried about gout. Lentz worried about whether hotel water would ruin a glaze test she'd left soaking at home.

Ordinary thoughts kept surfacing. Ordinary thoughts kept clearing people by accident. It should have made the room feel safer. It didn't. It only proved how many covers ordinary life could wear.

The quartet brightened. Glasses clinked. Somebody laughed too loudly near the terrace doors. Somewhere in the middle of all that, a traitor had gone silent and only Anya knew the hunt had started.

She drank the water down, set the empty glass aside, and went to find her coat with the phrase still lodged under her ribs.
state_deltas_required: assassination attempt on Damian; survival followed by proximity and emotional vulnerability; the recognition nearly completes
Physical danger survived together. The emotional boundary is crossed — a kiss, an intimacy neither can undo. Anya's telepathy during intimacy creates a moral problem. The recognition is now imminent.
